title: Blood in Cat Urine (Hematuria): Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has dark or bloody urine, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why this happens and what steps you need to take right now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bloody or dark urine in cats is a high-urgency symptom that requires immediate veterinary attention to rule out life-threatening conditions like urinary blockages.

What does it mean if my cat has blood in its urine?

Dark or bloody urine in cats, medically known as hematuria, is the presence of red blood cells in the urine. This can appear as pinkish fluid, bright red spots, or dark, tea-colored liquid. It is usually a symptom of severe inflammation, infection, or injury within the urinary tract, including the bladder or kidneys.

Is bloody urine in a cat a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for this symptom is High; you should contact a veterinarian immediately.
  • While it can be caused by a simple infection, it is often a sign of Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) or a life-threatening urinary blockage.
  • In male cats, a blockage can become fatal within 24 to 48 hours if the cat is unable to pass urine.
  • If your cat is straining, crying, or repeatedly visiting the litter box, seek emergency care now.

How can a photo of my cat's bloody urine help with triage?

  • A clear photo helps the medical team see the exact color and the presence of any blood clots.
  • Visual evidence provides information on the volume of urine passed.
  • This helps the vet determine the severity of the irritation and prioritize your cat's care during the triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with hematuria, potential underlying causes such as urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, urolithiasis, and sterile cystitis (feline interstitial cystitis) should be considered. Urolithiasis is usually suspected based on clinical signs of hematuria, dysuria, or urethral obstruction. Diagnostic evaluation may include urinalysis, urine culture, radiography, and ultrasonography to differentiate uroliths from other conditions. Radiography, cystoscopy, or ultrasonography are critically important to detect uroliths, as abdominal palpation has limited sensitivity. Urethral obstruction is an emergency, leading to uremia within 36-48 hours and potentially death within 72 hours; immediate treatment is crucial.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1531)

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: Because urinary issues can escalate to a total blockage very quickly, it is not recommended to wait. If your cat cannot urinate at all, it is a critical emergency.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for straining in the litter box, vocalizing (crying) while peeing, excessive grooming of the genital area, or urinating in unusual places like the sink or bathtub.

faqs.question: Is blood in urine common in cats?
faqs.answer: It is a common sign of stress-induced cystitis or bladder stones, but it is never considered 'normal' and always requires a veterinary evaluation to rule out life-threatening issues.
